EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system ejected eastward into the central part of the country just after Christmas. Precipitation started out as snow, but as warm air aloft moved in it switched over to liquid rain, freezing on contact with all sub-freezing surfaces at ground level.  The worst of the  freezing rain formed generally along the HWY 36 corridor, producing numerous power outages and causing several vehicle accidents. One such accident in Macon county proved fatal as a car slid off an icy road and killed one of its occupants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the day on December 29, a potent winter storm moved into the area. The precipitation started as primarily snow during the morning hours producing a couple inches of accumulation, but switched to freezing rain just before 10 am as warm air aloft moved over the area. Moderate, to at times heavy rain ensued through the rest of the morning and early to middle afternoon hours, before eventually moving out by the evening hours. Due to the rain rates, not all of the nearly 1 inch of liquid precipitation accreted on surfaces, but a quarter to half inch did accrete, causing a significant disruption to the power, and closing numerous roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The river flood season got off to an early start as the year 2020 opened. Rather mild and wet conditions continued into the new year. Rainfall totals across the region were 1 to over 3 inches above normal for January. At Cape Girardeau, Missouri, it was the 4th wettest January on record with a total of 6.52 inches. The subtropical jetstream was very active, drawing in moisture from the eastern Pacific and the Gulf of Mexico. This provided very heavy rainfall, especially during the first half of the month. Heavy rainfall on the 11th caused many of the region's rivers to rise rapidly. The mainstem Mississippi  River was already high and just began rising again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Mississippi River. The river crested at 37.49 feet during the pre-dawn hours of the 15th at the Cape Girardeau river gage. Some low-lying farmland near the river was inundated. The flood gate on Themis Street in Cape Girardeau was closed. The Mississippi River backed into several creeks, producing flooding well away from the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV combined with a weak cold front produced storms across the region. Some of the storms became severe and produced two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, weak tornado touched down in the early evening just to the northwest of Cuba, Missouri. The tornado first touched down to the west of Indian Lake, along Lakeshore Drive. The tornado did some minor damage to a roof on the west side of Lakeshore Drive. The worst of the damage occurred to two residences on the east side of Lakeshore Drive, on the west side of Indian Lake. A few trees were uprooted or snapped, and a home lost the majority of its roof. Additionally, a boat dock was completely flipped over. The tornado moved east across the lake, snapping a few other trees and doing some minor roof damage to a home along Indian Hills Drive. The tornado quickly lifted just before it hit Seminole Road. Additional tree damage and some minor house damage (a few shingles and siding peeled off) was noted along DD Highway well to the south and east of the damage near the lake. This damage was deemed to be straight line wind damage, caused by a surge in the rear flank downdraft of the thunderstorm. Overall the tornado was rated EF0 with a path length of 0.76 miles. The max path width was 100 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported with the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft brought several upper level disturbances to the region which interacted with a cold air mass to produce periods of rain and freezing rain from the evening of the 16th to the early morning of the 18th. Widespread precipitation accumulations of one to two inches on top of saturated grounds from the previous week led to flooding of many low water crossings and several state highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall caused Bennett Spring Creek at Bennett Spring State Park to exceed flood stage and close the Park and some roads in and near the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold season quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) moved across the Mid-South during the overnight and early morning hours Saturday, January 11, 2020. Strong southerly winds drew unseasonably warm, moist air northward into the region ahead of a cold front. Early morning temperatures were in the upper 60s and lower 70s with dewpoints in the upper 60s. This aided in the development of uncapped surface-based instability that was more than sufficient to maintain the QLCS. A strengthening  shortwave trough was lifting northeastward across the Ozarks during the event resulting in strengthing wind fields and ample wind shear.  The QLCS evolved into a Line Echo Wave Pattern (LEWP) with several persistent mesovortices across north Mississippi and a few transient mesovortices farther north into west Tennessee. Widespread damaging wind was observed across the entire County Warning Area (CWA) along with 13 tornadoes. In addition to the severe weather potential, heavy rainfall was a noted threat. Isolated flash flooding did occur with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into the Mid-South during the evening hours of April 8, 2020, into the early morning hours of April 9, 2020. The front intersected an area of moderate instability and strong wind shear. As a result, storms developed across eastern Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel then tracked across the Mid-South. Initially, storms were supercellular in nature with large hail and a couple of weak tornadoes. Storms eventually transitioned into clusters and line segments as they moved across the Mississippi River into West Tenessee and North Mississippi. These storms continued to produce large hail and damaging winds.
A strong cold front moved through the region triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and a couple of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree, tree limb and power line damage between Lake St. Louis, Dardenne Prairie and St. Peters. A couple of trees fell across a roadway about 4 miles south of Dardenne Prairie. Several power lines were knocked down at the intersection of Sommers Road and Highway N. Also, an 8 inch diameter tree limb fell onto a house in St. Peters causing minor roof and gutter damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed and moved through parts of the Missouri Bootheel and Northeast Arkansas producing sub-severe wind gusts and some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree knocked down in Senath.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow accumulation of freezing rain culminated in around a quarter inch of ice on surfaces in and around the Kansas City metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell through the morning, culminating in around a quarter inch of freezing rain. Temperatures rose above freezing by the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe storms developed from the evening of the 1st to the early morning of the 3rd as a surface low pressure system tracked from western Kansas southeastward into Red River Valley. Heavy rainfall occurred during the early morning of the 2nd when training storms moved over southwest Missouri producing one to three inches of rain. Flash flooding was reported from Jasper County eastward into Lawrence, Christian, Douglas and Webster Counties. The most severe storms developed during the afternoon of the 2nd over south central Missouri and produced hail up to one and a half inches in diameter in Dent and Shannon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent large upper level trough over the southwest U.S. ejected many upper level disturbances that interacted with weak surface boundaries and deep moisture over the region. On the 19th, strong storms and heavy rainfall occurred. Many flash flood reports were received as soils were saturated from earlier rainfall. A few storms produced damaging wind gusts over south central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway FF was closed due to flooding at Panther Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms affected the region from the morning of the 27th to the afternoon of the 28th. The most impactful storms occurred during the afternoon of the 27th as storms moving into the Ozarks from northeast Oklahoma were able to intensify due to an unstable airmass residing over the region. Very large hail occurred with the storms as they moved into south central Missouri during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported.

Very large hail occurred with the storms as they moved into south central Missouri during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of hail from ping pong ball to tennis ball size was reported as a storm moved through Hartville to the east. Numerous vehicles, businesses and residential roofs sustained damage as the storm proceeded from just west of town to the east with the last report of hail occurring near the intersection of Highway 38 and Lawson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow accumulation of freezing rain culminated in around a quarter inch of ice on surfaces in and around the Kansas City metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell through the morning, culminating in around a quarter inch of freezing rain. Temperatures rose above freezing by the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe storms developed from the evening of the 1st to the early morning of the 3rd as a surface low pressure system tracked from western Kansas southeastward into Red River Valley. Heavy rainfall occurred during the early morning of the 2nd when training storms moved over southwest Missouri producing one to three inches of rain. Flash flooding was reported from Jasper County eastward into Lawrence, Christian, Douglas and Webster Counties. The most severe storms developed during the afternoon of the 2nd over south central Missouri and produced hail up to one and a half inches in diameter in Dent and Shannon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail slightly smaller than tennis balls was reported east of Eminence off of State Highway V. The report was received via social media as a photo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent large upper level trough over the southwest U.S. ejected many upper level disturbances that interacted with weak surface boundaries and deep moisture over the region. On the 19th, strong storms and heavy rainfall occurred. Many flash flood reports were received as soils were saturated from earlier rainfall. A few storms produced damaging wind gusts over south central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cologna Road at the Pomme De Terre River was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant MCV moved across the region triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down two large tree limbs at intersection of Oregon Trail Drive and Woodstream Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant MCV moved across the region triggering showers and storms. Some of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds and large hail.

A mid season winter storm moved through the area bringing nearly every precipitation type with it. This storm produced around a quarter to half inch of ice and several inches of snow area-wide. There were several accidents, but at least one accident in Harrison County resulted in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred through much of the night going into January 11, and caused around a quarter to one-third inch accumulation. This occurred prior to about 2 to 3 inches of snow falling. This resulted in several auto accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of storm activity affected the region during the morning hours. Storms that developed over western Kansas on the evening of the 23rd tracked eastward into southeast Kansas and western Missouri during the morning of the 24th. Another area of storm development occurred near the Arkansas border north of a warm front. These storms congealed into a large complex of storms that tracked eastward across central Missouri through late morning. Rainfall accumulations over an inch occurred over most of the region, with isolated amounts over one and a half inches over southwest Missouri near Joplin, Neosho and Monett.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route D was closed due to flooding at a low water area south of Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered over south central Illinois dragged a weak cold front slowly south through the forecast area. Widespread convection developed along the Missouri River in central Missouri, dumping up to 4 inches of rain over a three hour period causing isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to four inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded across the southern portions of Cole County, including over the western portions of Brazito.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low centered over Missouri moved slowly eastward with two lobes of vorticity that were pivoting around each other. The interaction of the two vort maxes produced two distinct areas of heavy rain, one over central/east central Missouri and another over south central and southeast Missouri. Very heavy rain fell in these areas, mainly between 3 and 4 inches of rain, though a few areas had around 6 to 7 inches of rain with this system. There were several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing widespread flash flooding in northeastern Gasconade County. Numerous roads were flooded including Highway 19 and roads throughout Hermann.  Also, Frene Creek near the Adam Putcha Winery was well out of its banks with at least 3 to 4 feet of water flowing over Frene Creek Road just south of the intersection with Highway 100.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon, a line of thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall. These storms occurred just south of a warm front under a very strong southwest low level flow, around 60 knots at 850 mb. An upper level disturbance over the Ozark Mountains contributed to the storms. During the night of the 19th, another round of thunderstorms occurred in a strong southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front over western Missouri. These storms resulted in isolated flash flooding and minor flooding of a couple rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at EE Highway near County Road 238.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe storms developed from the evening of the 1st to the early morning of the 3rd as a surface low pressure system tracked from western Kansas southeastward into Red River Valley. A few storms produced damaging wind gusts over south central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway Y was closed due to flooding at the Gill Branch.

Numerous roads were flooded including State Highway D at intersection with County Road 947, County Road 917 south of Highway D, County Road 919 north of Highway D, and intersection of Highways T and E. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of discrete thunderstorms, a few of which displayed supercell characteristics, moved east across southeast Missouri during the evening. A couple of the storms produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls, and an EF-0 tornado was observed in southern New Madrid County. This outbreak of storms occurred in a strong west-southwest flow ahead of a 500 mb shortwave over the Plains states. A southwest wind flow up to 30 knot at 850 mb provided some moisture for the storms. At the surface, a low pressure center moved east-northeast from the Ozarks to the lower Ohio Valley. A frontal boundary extending east-northeast from the low provided a focus for the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous rain events led to renewed flooding of the Mississippi River in March. Minor to moderate flooding occurred. Monthly rainfall was 2 to 4 inches above normal for most of the area. Wet soil conditions contributed to runoff occurring fairly quickly with heavy rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Mississippi River. At the Cape Girardeau river gage, the river crested at 37.31 feet on the 26th. Flood stage is 32 feet. Some low-lying farmland near the river was inundated. The flood gate on Themis Street in Cape Girardeau was closed. The Mississippi River backed into several creeks, producing flooding well away from the river.The flooding continued into April.

A supercell thunderstorm intensified over the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri and weakened as it crossed the city of Poplar Bluff. This supercell thunderstorm reached its maximum intensity in northwest Butler County, between the cities of Ellsinore and Poplar Bluff. At its strongest, the storm spawned two weak tornadoes and baseball-size hail. The storm formed ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over central Missouri. Steep mid-level lapse rates of 8.5 degrees C/km were sampled by the 18z sounding from Springfield, MO. These steep lapse rates favored large hail. Dew points in the low 60's and afternoon heating contributed to a corridor of 1000 to 1500 mixed-layer capes along and ahead of the front. This moderate instability, combined with effective wind shear magnitudes of 50 to 60 knots, favored supercell development.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was roof damage to a convenience store on the north side of Poplar Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of discrete thunderstorms, a few of which displayed supercell characteristics, moved east across southeast Missouri during the evening. A couple of the storms produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls, and an EF-0 tornado was observed in southern New Madrid County. This outbreak of storms occurred in a strong west-southwest flow ahead of a 500 mb shortwave over the Plains states. A southwest wind flow up to 30 knot at 850 mb provided some moisture for the storms. At the surface, a low pressure center moved east-northeast from the Ozarks to the lower Ohio Valley. A frontal boundary extending east-northeast from the low provided a focus for the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofing material was removed from a house and thrown into a nearby field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe storms developed from the evening of the 1st to the early morning of the 3rd as a surface low pressure system tracked from western Kansas southeastward into Red River Valley. Heavy rainfall occurred during the early morning of the 2nd when training storms moved over southwest Missouri producing one to three inches of rain. Flash flooding was reported from Jasper County eastward into Lawrence, Christian, Douglas and Webster Counties. The most severe storms developed during the afternoon of the 2nd over south central Missouri and produced hail up to one and a half inches in diameter in Dent and Shannon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail was reported.

An MCV combined with a weak cold front produced storms across the region. Some of the storms became severe and produced two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado rapidly developed near Hillsboro Road to the east of Park Hills, Missouri. This tornado initially caused sporadic tree damage to a grove of trees west of Hillsboro Road. As it approached Hillsboro Road, the tornado widened and became a bit stronger, causing EF-1 damage to a home by collapsing its southward facing exterior wall. Minor roof damage also occurred to another home, along with several trees that were snapped. The tornado then crossed Hillsboro Road and did some minor roof damage to an outbuilding on the east side of the roadway. The tornado lifted as it approached a large grove of trees on the eastern side of Hillsboro Road. Overall, the tornado was rated EF1 with a path length of 0.74 miles. The max path width was 125 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms were strong to severe with several reports of damaging winds. Also, several rounds of heavy rain fell across the region in a two day period which caused flash flooding at a couple of locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms moved through Reynolds County. There were numerous reports of trees and power lines blown down, especially between  Centerville and the county line with Iron County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog reduced visibility to one-quarter mile or less. The dense fog occurred to the north of a warm front that was perched just south of the area. The fog dissipated as the front lifted north early in the morning.

A persistent large upper level trough over the southwest U.S. ejected many upper level disturbances that interacted with weak surface boundaries and deep moisture over the region. On the 19th, strong storms and heavy rainfall occurred. Many flash flood reports were received as soils were saturated from earlier rainfall. A few storms produced damaging wind gusts over south central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Finely River was flowing out of its banks and into a riverside park in Ozarks. One water rescue occurred with the subject rescued safely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low centered over Missouri moved slowly eastward with two lobes of vorticity that were pivoting around each other. The interaction of the two vort maxes produced two distinct areas of heavy rain, one over central/east central Missouri and another over south central and southeast Missouri. Very heavy rain fell in these areas, mainly between 3 and 4 inches of rain, though a few areas had around 6 to 7 inches of rain with this system. There were several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Baileys Creek was out of its banks about 3 miles southwest of Morrison, flooding sections of County Road 275.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm intensified over the Ozark foothills of southeast Missouri and weakened as it crossed the city of Poplar Bluff. This supercell thunderstorm reached its maximum intensity in northwest Butler County, between the cities of Ellsinore and Poplar Bluff. At its strongest, the storm spawned two weak tornadoes and baseball-size hail. The storm formed ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over central Missouri. Steep mid-level lapse rates of 8.5 degrees C/km were sampled by the 18z sounding from Springfield, MO. These steep lapse rates favored large hail. Dew points in the low 60's and afternoon heating contributed to a corridor of 1000 to 1500 mixed-layer capes along and ahead of the front. This moderate instability, combined with effective wind shear magnitudes of 50 to 60 knots, favored supercell development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of discrete thunderstorms, a few of which displayed supercell characteristics, moved east across southeast Missouri during the evening. A couple of the storms produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls, and an EF-0 tornado was observed in southern New Madrid County. This outbreak of storms occurred in a strong west-southwest flow ahead of a 500 mb shortwave over the Plains states. A southwest wind flow up to 30 knot at 850 mb provided some moisture for the storms. At the surface, a low pressure center moved east-northeast from the Ozarks to the lower Ohio Valley. A frontal boundary extending east-northeast from the low provided a focus for the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two small but intense bowing thunderstorm clusters moved rapidly east/southeastward across southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. Damage surveys confirmed a brief EF-0 tornado, however the most noteworthy events were two macrobursts containing peak winds in the 95 to 110 mph range. Flash flooding of low water crossings  occurred due to repeated thunderstorm activity over the Ozark foothills. Although the bowing clusters of storms resided in relatively stable air to the north of a stationary front over western Tennessee and central Arkansas, occasional damaging wind gusts occurred due to their well-organized nature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ponding of water was reported on low-lying streets in Poplar Bluff. Water partially covered roads west of Harviell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few slow-moving clusters of storms produced heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding.  The storms occurred within a moist southerly wind flow ahead of a cold front over the Plains states.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street and road flooding occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of May 3, 2020 brought a marginally severe storm into portions of east central Kansas and west central Missouri. This storm was somewhat isolated in the damage it caused, but the worsts of this two day event occurred the next day. The morning of May 4 started out incredibly active across eastern Kansas and western Missouri. In eastern Kansas several supercells formed and moved eastward into western Missouri. The initial round of supercells were generally decaying as they progressed eastward, but one particular supercell formed an enhanced downburst in western Bates County and produced between 70 and 90 mph winds. Upstream of this supercell in eastern Kansas 15-20 power poles were snapped near the intersection known as Jingo, KS. This storm then went on to produce heavy damage to trees and structures between Amsterdam and Butler. It was in this area just west of Passaic that a single fatality occurred when a tree fell into a house. After this storm moved out of the area a follow-up bow pushed through the same area producing pockets of strong winds that were recorded up to nearly 80 mph at more than one ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of training thunderstorms set up across southern Missouri. Several rounds of storms developed over central Kansas and tracked southeastward into the Ozarks as moisture lifted up and over a warm front over the Red River Valley. Many low water crossings flooded over southern Missouri. Highway C along Flat Creek in eastern Barry County and Highway 97 at the Spring River were closed due to flooding. Rainfall accumulations exceeded two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 95 was closed at Bryant Creek due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass interacted with several upper level disturbances and a very slow moving upper level low pressure system to bring heavy rain to the region. The storms occurring on the afternoon of the 14th produced hail larger than golf balls and damaging straight-line winds from Springfield eastward into West Plains. With grounds already saturated from the rainfall on the 11th and 12th,  widespread stream flooding and flash flooding developed, mainly on the 16th and 17, after rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were reported down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along parts of the Mississippi River, minor flooding occurred during early May in response to a few rounds of thunderstorms from late April into early May. The gage at Cape Girardeau had been above flood stage since mid March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor river flooding occurred in the Cape Girardeau area. Low-lying fields, a few river access roads, and some riverfront parkland was under water. Some crop damage occurred to early planted crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaver Creek near Joe Hollow was rushing over Highway 125 at the low water crossing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Y at the low water crossing on Cowskin Creek was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado accompanied a squall line of thunderstorms that moved rapidly east-southeast. The wind damage was mainly along and south of a line from Van Buren to Greenville to Sikeston. The atmosphere was moderately unstable, with cape values on the order of 1500 to 2000 J/kg. The relatively compact but organized and vigorous convective system maintained intensity as it progressed east to the Mississippi River. The damaging wind events occurred along and south of a surface frontal boundary, where dew points were in the mid 60's.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down across the county. People were trapped by falling trees in a campground near Van Buren. Power outages were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-1 tornado damaged or destroyed several farm buildings. The farm buildings included large metal storage buildings. The concrete anchors of a shed were pulled from the ground. The tornado cut through the city of Marston, where the city hall and an apartment building were partially unroofed. At least three power poles were broken. Numerous trees were uprooted. The discernable tornado path ended in the Mississippi River flood plain. Peak winds were estimated near 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of May 3, 2020 brought a marginally severe storm into portions of east central Kansas and west central Missouri. This storm was somewhat isolated in the damage it caused, but the worsts of this two day event occurred the next day. The morning of May 4 started out incredibly active across eastern Kansas and western Missouri. In eastern Kansas several supercells formed and moved eastward into western Missouri. The initial round of supercells were generally decaying as they progressed eastward, but one particular supercell formed an enhanced downburst in western Bates County and produced between 70 and 90 mph winds. Upstream of this supercell in eastern Kansas 15-20 power poles were snapped near the intersection known as Jingo, KS. This storm then went on to produce heavy damage to trees and structures between Amsterdam and Butler. It was in this area just west of Passaic that a single fatality occurred when a tree fell into a house. After this storm moved out of the area a follow-up bow pushed through the same area producing pockets of strong winds that were recorded up to nearly 80 mph at more than one ASOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 60 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of training thunderstorms set up across southern Missouri. Several rounds of storms developed over central Kansas and tracked southeastward into the Ozarks as moisture lifted up and over a warm front over the Red River Valley. Many low water crossings flooded over southern Missouri. Highway C along Flat Creek in eastern Barry County and Highway 97 at the Spring River were closed due to flooding. Rainfall accumulations exceeded two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway C was closed at Flat Creek due to flooding.

Two small but intense bowing thunderstorm clusters moved rapidly east/southeastward across southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. Damage surveys confirmed a brief EF-0 tornado, however the most noteworthy events were two macrobursts containing peak winds in the 95 to 110 mph range. Flash flooding of low water crossings  occurred due to repeated thunderstorm activity over the Ozark foothills. Although the bowing clusters of storms resided in relatively stable air to the north of a stationary front over western Tennessee and central Arkansas, occasional damaging wind gusts occurred due to their well-organized nature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of many low water crossings in northern Ripley County made them impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two small but intense bowing thunderstorm clusters moved rapidly east/southeastward across southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. Damage surveys confirmed a brief EF-0 tornado, however the most noteworthy events were two macrobursts containing peak winds in the 95 to 110 mph range. Flash flooding of low water crossings  occurred due to repeated thunderstorm activity over the Ozark foothills. Although the bowing clusters of storms resided in relatively stable air to the north of a stationary front over western Tennessee and central Arkansas, occasional damaging wind gusts occurred due to their well-organized nature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very intense macroburst caused extensive damage across southwest New Madrid County, including the Gideon area. Peak winds were estimated near 110 mph, based on NWS storm surveys. About eight miles of the 19-mile long damage swath was in New Madrid County. Most of the remainder of the path was in Pemiscot County. The width of the macroburst averaged about six miles in New Madrid County. Several large grain bins were destroyed, with some blown a hundred yards or more from their anchored position. A couple of large storage buildings collapsed or partially collapsed. Several garages, farm sheds and similar structures were heavily damaged or destroyed. At least a half dozen homes or similar structures sustained major roof damage, ranging from the removal of the roof decking structure to removal of the entire roof structure. One had the attached garage separated. At least one home was destroyed by a fallen tree, with several others damaged from fallen trees. Several miles of power lines were blown down as poles were broken. Numerous large trees were uprooted. Much of the damage was in and south of Gideon. At least three homes and a business sustained major roof damage in Gideon, and a large farm building near Highway 162 lost portions of its roof and walls. The local emergency management agency estimated at least 5 million dollars damage, with 4 million of that at a farm south of Gideon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread frost occurred over southeast Missouri except the extreme southeast counties east of Poplar Bluff. A strong high pressure system was centered over the Ozark Mountains early on the 9th, resulting in clear skies and chilly northwest winds. The winds were light enough for a brief period of frost in most places. Low temperatures were in the mid 30's, with even a few lower 30's north and west of Marble Hill. The main impact of the frost was on horticulture and fruit crops.

A moist and unstable air mass interacted with several upper level disturbances and a very slow moving upper level low pressure system to bring heavy rain to the region. The storms occurring on the afternoon of the 14th produced hail larger than golf balls and damaging straight-line winds from Springfield eastward into West Plains. With grounds already saturated from the rainfall on the 11th and 12th,  widespread stream flooding and flash flooding developed, mainly on the 16th and 17, after rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals over a 24 hour period were measured at 5.50 inches in the town of Summersville and 4.02 at a CoCoRAHS site 3 miles south of town.

With grounds already saturated from the rainfall on the 11th and 12th,  widespread stream flooding and flash flooding developed, mainly on the 16th and 17, after rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations along Highway 125 east of Sparta were flooded and impassable including location that do not normally flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of wind damage and an isolated tornado accompanied a squall line of thunderstorms that moved rapidly east-southeast. The wind damage was mainly along and south of a line from Van Buren to Greenville to Sikeston. The atmosphere was moderately unstable, with cape values on the order of 1500 to 2000 J/kg. The relatively compact but organized and vigorous convective system maintained intensity as it progressed east to the Mississippi River. The damaging wind events occurred along and south of a surface frontal boundary, where dew points were in the mid 60's.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five miles north of Oxly, a trained spotter measured a wind gust to 58 mph.

Two small but intense bowing thunderstorm clusters moved rapidly east/southeastward across southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. Damage surveys confirmed a brief EF-0 tornado, however the most noteworthy events were two macrobursts containing peak winds in the 95 to 110 mph range. Flash flooding of low water crossings  occurred due to repeated thunderstorm activity over the Ozark foothills. Although the bowing clusters of storms resided in relatively stable air to the north of a stationary front over western Tennessee and central Arkansas, occasional damaging wind gusts occurred due to their well-organized nature.

A band of training thunderstorms set up across southern Missouri. Several rounds of storms developed over central Kansas and tracked southeastward into the Ozarks as moisture lifted up and over a warm front over the Red River Valley. Many low water crossings flooded over southern Missouri. Highway C along Flat Creek in eastern Barry County and Highway 97 at the Spring River were closed due to flooding. Rainfall accumulations exceeded two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 97 was closed at the Spring River due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small clusters of thunderstorms moved northward from Arkansas, producing heavy rain. One of the strongest storms produced large hail and isolated wind damage in the Ozark foothills. The storms occurred just ahead of a cold front that extended from the upper Mississippi Valley to the Ozark plateau of western Missouri. The moist southerly wind flow was accompanied by dew points in the mid 60's, contributing to weak instability sufficient for active convection.

Two small but intense bowing thunderstorm clusters moved rapidly east/southeastward across southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. Damage surveys confirmed a brief EF-0 tornado, however the most noteworthy events were two macrobursts containing peak winds in the 95 to 110 mph range. Flash flooding of low water crossings  occurred due to repeated thunderstorm activity over the Ozark foothills. Although the bowing clusters of storms resided in relatively stable air to the north of a stationary front over western Tennessee and central Arkansas, occasional damaging wind gusts occurred due to their well-organized nature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down across C Highway south of U.S. Highway 60.

Damage surveys confirmed a brief EF-0 tornado, however the most noteworthy events were two macrobursts containing peak winds in the 95 to 110 mph range. Flash flooding of low water crossings  occurred due to repeated thunderstorm activity over the Ozark foothills. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A macroburst containing winds estimated up to 95 mph produced major damage across extreme northern Ripley County. The path in Ripley County was nearly 19 miles long and nearly five miles wide. Thousands of large trees were snapped or uprooted. Numerous power lines were blown down or downed by falling trees, causing the worst power outage in the area since the 2009 ice storm. Approximately 30 structures, mainly barns and garages, were damaged or destroyed. Several houses sustained mainly minor roof damage, with one having several windows blown out. Multiple trees were down on houses. Roads were blocked by fallen trees. A wind gust to 63 mph was measured north of Bennett, where large trees were uprooted.

Damage surveys confirmed a brief EF-0 tornado, however the most noteworthy events were two macrobursts containing peak winds in the 95 to 110 mph range. Flash flooding of low water crossings  occurred due to repeated thunderstorm activity over the Ozark foothills. Although the bowing clusters of storms resided in relatively stable air to the north of a stationary front over western Tennessee and central Arkansas, occasional damaging wind gusts occurred due to their well-organized nature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated observing system at the Poplar Bluff airport measured a wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of May 3, 2020 brought a marginally severe storm into portions of east central Kansas and west central Missouri. This storm was somewhat isolated in the damage it caused, but the worsts of this two day event occurred the next day. The morning of May 4 started out incredibly active across eastern Kansas and western Missouri. In eastern Kansas several supercells formed and moved eastward into western Missouri. The initial round of supercells were generally decaying as they progressed eastward, but one particular supercell formed an enhanced downburst in western Bates County and produced between 70 and 90 mph winds. Upstream of this supercell in eastern Kansas 15-20 power poles were snapped near the intersection known as Jingo, KS. This storm then went on to produce heavy damage to trees and structures between Amsterdam and Butler. It was in this area just west of Passaic that a single fatality occurred when a tree fell into a house. After this storm moved out of the area a follow-up bow pushed through the same area producing pockets of strong winds that were recorded up to nearly 80 mph at more than one ASOS.

Two rounds of severe weather formed on May 25th. The first round produced a weak and brief tornado that was witnessed, but produced no damage. The second round later in the evening mainly produced some scattered minor flash flooding events and strong winds that produced minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least two reports of a brief tornado came to the office, one of which was from an off duty National Weather Service employee. No damage was reported from this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering heavy rains continued to produce flash flooding and areal flooding across the region as slow moving storms moved over areas that had received over 3 inches of rain in some instances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dalton Road was closed at the Brown Branch Tributary low water crossing due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Small clusters of thunderstorms moved northward from Arkansas, producing heavy rain. One of the strongest storms produced large hail and isolated wind damage in the Ozark foothills. The storms occurred just ahead of a cold front that extended from the upper Mississippi Valley to the Ozark plateau of western Missouri. The moist southerly wind flow was accompanied by dew points in the mid 60's, contributing to weak instability sufficient for active convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down, and one shed was destroyed due to a fallen tree. There was also a little damage to the roof of a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms moved rapidly southeast ahead of a strong 500 mb shortwave trough digging southeast across the Plains states. A couple of damaging wind reports were received along this line of storms. The storms occurred in a region of moderately strong wind shear ahead of a cold front moving east across Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tractor trailers were moved by wind in Poynor. Several small trees were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms with moderate to heavy rainfall occurred as two storm systems tracked northeastward from west Texas into the mid-Mississippi Valley. The repeated rounds of rainfall eventually led to flooding of low water crossings, creeks and a few of the larger rivers. Rainfall accumulations for the event  ranged from around an inch over south central Missouri to over three inches near the Missouri-Kansas border near Joplin and Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway V was closed at Weaubleau Creek due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked along a warm front stretched just north of the Mid-South. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level disturbance tracked into the region increasing wind fields and triggering thunderstorms along the warm front and south of the front, in the warm sector, during the late evening hours of March 2, 2020.  A couple of supercells tracked across the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee during the late evening hours. One supercell spawned three tornadoes across Gibson, Carroll, and Benton counties. A few severe storms also developed along a trailing cold front across eastern Arkansas and West Tennessee during the early morning hours of March 3, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line winds knocked over an empty rail car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow mesoscale band of heavy snow formed across far northern Missouri and southern Iowa late in the day on April 16 and carried into the early morning hours on April 17. Light snow fell prior to the heavier snow, but that only accounted for about an inch of the otherwise 6 to 12 inches of snow reported across the warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell off and on through the day on Thursday, accumulating about an inch; however, by mid to late afternoon the snow picked up intensity. One to two inch per hour snow rates were reported across the area for periods. Numerous reports of very low visibility due to very heavy snow were also received. The heavier snow came to an end on the evening of April 16, and gradually tapered to a stop by early morning on April 17. When all was said and done there was about 6 to 8 inches of snow reported across portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous rain events in March led to renewed flooding of the Mississippi River. Monthly rainfall was 2 to 4 inches above normal in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding was observed on the Mississippi River. At the New Madrid gage, the river crested at 40.17 feet early on April 1. Flood stage there is 34 feet. Considerable flooding of low-lying fields and farmland occurred, in addition to some bottomland secondary roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under a narrow ridge of high pressure, clear skies and light winds were conducive for widespread freezing temperatures. Observed low temperatures on the morning of the 15th ranged from the upper 20's to mid 30's. The mid 30's were confined to areas along the Arkansas border from Poplar Bluff west, where frost occurred in the absence of freezing temperatures. Fruit crops were damaged. The degree of damage was dependent on the site, crop, and variety of fruit. In particular, apples, peaches, and grapes were vulnerable to a freeze. The asparagus harvest was stopped by the freeze. Freeze-sensitive perennials in gardens were green and actively growing when the freeze occurred.

Severe thunderstorms occurred during the late afternoon and evening hours as a strong cold front moved southeast into the area. Surface low pressure moved across central Illinois during the late afternoon hours. High temperatures soared into the mid 80's in many locations, which helped to prime the atmosphere for severe thunderstorm development. Surface-based instability was quite strong. Very cold air aloft contributed to steep mid-level lapse rates near 8.5 C/km, very favorable for large hail. Clusters of severe thunderstorms moved east-southeast across southeast Missouri, accompanied by sporadic large hail and very isolated wind damage. The strongest storm originated near the northeast corner of Wayne County and moved down across the Sikeston area to northern Mississippi County. This storm produced a nearly continuous swath of large hail from golf-ball size to about the size of hen eggs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree tops were broken off in Frohna and Altenburg. Live ten-inch tree trunks were snapped. Trampolines were moved. Power outages occurred on the west side of Altenburg.

On the afternoon of April 24, 2020 scattered convection formed across central Missouri. Early in the storm formation period one of these storms produced a brief landspout that was photographed by some passers-by. No damage was reported with this tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout formed near Malta Bend. After the office received a couple reports of a funnel cloud underneath new convection some photos were sent to the office via Twitter of a broad dust swirl that extended upward to the cloud base. No damage occurred as a result of this weak and brief landspout.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred during the late afternoon and evening hours as a strong cold front moved southeast into the area. Surface low pressure moved across central Illinois during the late afternoon hours. High temperatures soared into the mid 80's in many locations, which helped to prime the atmosphere for severe thunderstorm development. Surface-based instability was quite strong. Very cold air aloft contributed to steep mid-level lapse rates near 8.5 C/km, very favorable for large hail. Clusters of severe thunderstorms moved east-southeast across southeast Missouri, accompanied by sporadic large hail and very isolated wind damage. The strongest storm originated near the northeast corner of Wayne County and moved down across the Sikeston area to northern Mississippi County. This storm produced a nearly continuous swath of large hail from golf-ball size to about the size of hen eggs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced hail up to the size of quarters in and near Perryville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and evening as a dry line and cold front moved across the region and interacted with a very unstable air mass. The storms produced widespread straight-line wind damage from Columbus, Kansas to Springfield to Eminence. Rainfall accumulations over an inch were widespread across the area, with localized amounts over one and a half inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports were received of trees, power lines and poles down across the county from multiple sources. Winds of 60-75 mph were reported along the gust front of a line of severe thunderstorms. The ASOS at the Joplin Airport measured a 62 mph gust. Reports were received from the cities of Joplin, Jasper, Duquesne, Diamond, Carrytown and Sarcoxie. On I-49, at the Jasper exit, a Semi-Truck was overturned though no injuries were reported. Law enforcement also reported a possible funnel cloud west of Sarcoxie. No evidence of a tornado was found or reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and evening as a dry line and cold front moved across the region and interacted with a very unstable air mass. The storms produced widespread straight-line wind damage from Columbus, Kansas to Springfield to Eminence. Rainfall accumulations over an inch were widespread across the area, with localized amounts over one and a half inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports were received from multiple sources across the county. Winds were reported at 60 mph as a line of storms moved through the county. Reports were received from the cities of Miller, Aurora, Freistatt and Lawrenceburg. Multiple trees, large branches, power poles and lines were downed by the high winds. In the Miller fire district a downed line caused a grass small fire. Minor siding and shingle damage was reported to a few homes in Lawrenceburg. No injuries were reported.

A narrow mesoscale band of heavy snow formed across far northern Missouri and southern Iowa late in the day on April 16 and carried into the early morning hours on April 17. Light snow fell prior to the heavier snow, but that only accounted for about an inch of the otherwise 6 to 12 inches of snow reported across the warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell off and on through the day on Thursday, accumulating about an inch; however, by mid to late afternoon the snow picked up intensity. One to two inch per hour snow rates were reported across the area for periods. Numerous reports of very low visibility due to very heavy snow were also received. The heavier snow came to an end on the evening of April 16, and gradually tapered to a stop by early morning on April 17. When all was said and done there was about 6 to 10 inches of snow reported across portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 10th, strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front over eastern Oklahoma and southeast Kansas before spreading northeast into the Missouri Ozarks through the early morning of the 11th. Several tornadoes rated as EF-0 and EF-1 occurred near the towns of Strafford, Fair Play and Cross Timbers, and hail up to the size of golf balls occurred on the east side of Springfield. Widespread rainfall amounts of two to four inches led to flooding and closures of numerous county roads and state highways. Several swift water rescues were performed and unfortunately there was one flood-related fatality near Conway in Laclede County, when a 53-year old woman attempted to drive across a flooded low water crossing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported by the Public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong storm system impacted the region January 10-11. Heavy rainfall, flooding, isolated damaging winds, a couple tornadoes, and gusty non-thunderstorm winds impacted southeast Missouri during this time. The bulk of the event occurred overnight and into the morning hours on the 11th. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches, which resulted in isolated flash flooding. The most significant tornado occurred in the Jackson area, which was rated EF-2. Strong to severe convection over the Ozark Mountains moved east into an environment of very strong wind shear over southeast Missouri, with 850 mb winds near 60 knots. The convection maintained intensity after it crossed the Mississippi River during the early morning hours. Tremendous wind shear (winds of 50 knots down to 1500 feet above ground level) and strong forcing associated with a potent 500 mb shortwave compensated for weak instability. Some of the strong low-level winds reached the surface in the form of gradient wind gusts to around 40 mph. A weak surface low formed along a cold front over southwest Arkansas, then deepened as it tracked northeast across the Missouri Bootheel and into the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado briefly touched down along Highway 72. The workshop portion of a home-workshop combination building was destroyed. The roof was torn off a nearby barn. Debris was thrown to the east about 100 yards, to the south about 100 feet, and to the west less than 20 feet. A few trees were uprooted. Peak winds were estimated near 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong storm system impacted the region January 10-11. Heavy rainfall, flooding, isolated damaging winds, a couple tornadoes, and gusty non-thunderstorm winds impacted southeast Missouri during this time. The bulk of the event occurred overnight and into the morning hours on the 11th. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches, which resulted in isolated flash flooding. The most significant tornado occurred in the Jackson area, which was rated EF-2. Strong to severe convection over the Ozark Mountains moved east into an environment of very strong wind shear over southeast Missouri, with 850 mb winds near 60 knots. The convection maintained intensity after it crossed the Mississippi River during the early morning hours. Tremendous wind shear (winds of 50 knots down to 1500 feet above ground level) and strong forcing associated with a potent 500 mb shortwave compensated for weak instability. Some of the strong low-level winds reached the surface in the form of gradient wind gusts to around 40 mph. A weak surface low formed along a cold front over southwest Arkansas, then deepened as it tracked northeast across the Missouri Bootheel and into the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just south of U.S. Highway 61, where trees and small outbuildings were damaged. The tornado crossed Highway 61 and produced damage to several businesses. Windows were broken out, and damage occurred to roofs. One business was destroyed. A tire store was heavily damaged as the tornado was at its peak intensity. The back was wall was blown out, and much of the roof was destroyed and thrown several hundred yards. Light concrete and styrofoam blew onto Interstate 55, which was closed for a brief time until cleanup was complete. East of Interstate 55, several barns and outbuildings were damaged or destroyed. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped along the way. The path may have extended farther east of State Route 177 near the Mississippi River, but flooding prevented surveying. Peak winds were estimated near 125 mph near U.S. Highway 61.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted as high as 40 to 45 mph in southeast Missouri. The peak gust at the Poplar Bluff and Cape Girardeau airports was 41 mph. The strong winds occurred in the wake of a low pressure center moving east across western Kentucky.

Several swift water rescues were performed and unfortunately there was one flood-related fatality near Conway in Laclede County, when a 53-year old woman attempted to drive across a flooded low water crossing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey determined that an EF1 tornado touched down to the southwest of Fair Play in Cedar County and tracked to the northeast for a total of 8.8 miles, lifting west of Dunnegan in Polk County with maximum winds of 110 mph. The tornado caused tree damage as it tracked for a half mile northeast across extreme eastern Cedar County before crossing into Polk County along South 10th Road.  The tornado path then continued into Polk County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 10th, strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front over eastern Oklahoma and southeast Kansas before spreading northeast into the Missouri Ozarks through the early morning of the 11th. Several tornadoes rated as EF-0 and EF-1 occurred near the towns of Strafford, Fair Play and Cross Timbers, and hail up to the size of golf balls occurred on the east side of Springfield. Widespread rainfall amounts of two to four inches led to flooding and closures of numerous county roads and state highways. Several swift water rescues were performed and unfortunately there was one flood-related fatality near Conway in Laclede County, when a 53-year old woman attempted to drive across a flooded low water crossing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey determined that an EF1 tornado touched down to the southwest of Fair Play in Cedar County and tracked to the northeast for a total of 8.8 miles, lifting west of Dunnegan in Polk County with maximum winds of 110 mph. Shortly after touchdown and downing trees in Cedar County, the tornado completely destroyed a manufactured home as it crossed the county line at South 10th Road into Polk County. The tornado then continued tracking to the northeast. It passed just to the west and northwest of Fair Play before crossing Highway 123. The tornado lifted just to the west of Highway 13 and to the north of East 380th Road in Polk County. Along the path of the tornado there were several trees uprooted or snapped, damage to a few homes, and several outbuildings destroyed. The tornado produced EF1 damage with maximum winds up to 110 mph and a max width of 50 yards. The tornado was on the ground for 14 minutes along an 8.8 mile path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Mid-South during the day on March 24, 2020. The cold front intercepted a moist and unstable airmass resulting in the development of thunderstorms across Northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel. These thunderstorms gradually spread southeast into North Mississippi and intensified late in the afternoon. A tornado touched down in Tishomingo County, MS causing significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported between Senath and Kennett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure center tracked northeast across Indiana to the eastern Great Lakes region. In the wake of this intensifying storm system, northwest winds increased during the late night hours of the 12th. Winds gusted from 40 to 45 mph at many locations. The peak measured wind gust was 46 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport. Other peak gusts included 45 mph at the Poplar Bluff airport and 41 mph at the Perryville airport.

Severe weather with flooding rains occurred on January 10, with colder air moving into the region. This cold air intrusion then turned the rain to freezing drizzle and snow over portions of southern Missouri during the early morning hours of January 11. Anywhere from a glaze of ice to 1 to 4 inches of snow fell cross the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flat ice accumulation of 0.05 inches at the SGF ASOS. Also widespread 1 to 2 inches of snow across the county with several vehicle slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 10th, strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front over eastern Oklahoma and southeast Kansas before spreading northeast into the Missouri Ozarks through the early morning of the 11th. Several tornadoes rated as EF-0 and EF-1 occurred near the towns of Strafford, Fair Play and Cross Timbers, and hail up to the size of golf balls occurred on the east side of Springfield. Widespread rainfall amounts of two to four inches led to flooding and closures of numerous county roads and state highways. Several swift water rescues were performed and unfortunately there was one flood-related fatality near Conway in Laclede County, when a 53-year old woman attempted to drive across a flooded low water crossing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant roof damage was reported to a few homes.  Shingles were blown 100 to 200 yards along with many branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong storm system impacted the region January 10-11. Heavy rainfall, flooding, isolated damaging winds, a couple tornadoes, and gusty non-thunderstorm winds impacted southeast Missouri during this time. The bulk of the event occurred overnight and into the morning hours on the 11th. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches, which resulted in isolated flash flooding. The most significant tornado occurred in the Jackson area, which was rated EF-2. Strong to severe convection over the Ozark Mountains moved east into an environment of very strong wind shear over southeast Missouri, with 850 mb winds near 60 knots. The convection maintained intensity after it crossed the Mississippi River during the early morning hours. Tremendous wind shear (winds of 50 knots down to 1500 feet above ground level) and strong forcing associated with a potent 500 mb shortwave compensated for weak instability. Some of the strong low-level winds reached the surface in the form of gradient wind gusts to around 40 mph. A weak surface low formed along a cold front over southwest Arkansas, then deepened as it tracked northeast across the Missouri Bootheel and into the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of county roads was reported around Biehle. The co-operative observer at the Perryville water plant measured 3.03 inches in 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Survey determined that an EF-1 tornado touched down a few miles southeast of Cross Timbers on Highway CC and tracked for a mile and a half to the northeast before quickly lifting. Along the path of the tornado there were numerous trees snapped or uprooted, minor roof damage to two homes, and an outbuilding completely destroyed. The tornado produced EF1 damage with maximum winds up to 100 mph and a max width of 100 yards. The tornado was on the ground for 3 minutes along a 1.6 mile path.

During the afternoon and evening of the 10th, strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front over eastern Oklahoma and southeast Kansas before spreading northeast into the Missouri Ozarks through the early morning of the 11th. Several tornadoes rated as EF-0 and EF-1 occurred near the towns of Strafford, Fair Play and Cross Timbers, and hail up to the size of golf balls occurred on the east side of Springfield. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Survey determined that an EF-1 tornado touched down  east of Highway 65 on East Mill Street, just east of Springfield. The tornado tracked to the northeast crossing East Division Street. As the tornado approached Interstate 44, damage was sustained to an automobile service building. The service building lost a couple of overhead doors and two box trucks were overturned. Damage to the roof was also sustained. As it crossed Interstate 44 into Strafford, there was damage to several homes. The damage included a significant loss of roof covering material, damage to gutters, and collapse of garage doors inward. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted along the path. The tornado lifted just before Highway 125 near East Farm Road 84. The tornado produced EF1 damage with maximum winds up to 98 mph and a max width of 75 yards. The tornado was on the ground for 11 minutes along a 7 mile path.

Widespread rainfall amounts of two to four inches led to flooding and closures of numerous county roads and state highways. Several swift water rescues were performed and unfortunately there was one flood-related fatality near Conway in Laclede County, when a 53-year old woman attempted to drive across a flooded low water crossing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway HH is closed due to flooding at Ingalls Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong storm system impacted the region January 10-11. Heavy rainfall, flooding, isolated damaging winds, a couple tornadoes, and gusty non-thunderstorm winds impacted southeast Missouri during this time. The bulk of the event occurred overnight and into the morning hours on the 11th. Rainfall totals ranged from 2 to 5 inches, which resulted in isolated flash flooding. The most significant tornado occurred in the Jackson area, which was rated EF-2. Strong to severe convection over the Ozark Mountains moved east into an environment of very strong wind shear over southeast Missouri, with 850 mb winds near 60 knots. The convection maintained intensity after it crossed the Mississippi River during the early morning hours. Tremendous wind shear (winds of 50 knots down to 1500 feet above ground level) and strong forcing associated with a potent 500 mb shortwave compensated for weak instability. Some of the strong low-level winds reached the surface in the form of gradient wind gusts to around 40 mph. A weak surface low formed along a cold front over southwest Arkansas, then deepened as it tracked northeast across the Missouri Bootheel and into the lower Ohio Valley on the morning of the 11th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused a swath of damage across southern portions of the City of St. Louis, from the intersection of Utah Street and Nebraska Avenue to S. Broadway, just south of intersection with I-55. At least 12 homes and other structures sustained minor to moderate roof damage in this area, especially on Indiana Avenue near Cherokee Street. Also, numerous tree limbs and several power lines were blown down in this area. A couple of cars sustained damage from flying debris and falling trees. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River remained above flood stage through the month of April in the Cape Girardeau area. The Mississippi River first rose above flood stage at Cape Girardeau in mid March, and it remained above flood stage throughout April. April was somewhat drier than previous months, allowing the river to fall unsteadily from its late March crest. Details on the flooding impacts are contained in the March report. Impacts gradually decreased as floodwaters lessened in April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure center tracked northeast across Indiana to the eastern Great Lakes region. In the wake of this intensifying storm system, northwest winds increased during the late night hours of the 12th. Winds gusted from 40 to 45 mph at many locations. The peak measured wind gust was 46 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport. Other peak gusts included 45 mph at the Poplar Bluff airport and 41 mph at the Perryville airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe storms moved southeast across the region. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused moderate damage to several barns and a couple of trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped a power pole on West Main Street just south of town. Also, numerous tree limbs, as well as several trees were blown down on Grider Road and onto Rock Road just east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe storms moved southeast across the region. There were numerous reports of damaging winds and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees and caused minor damage to a barn north of Garwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of three rounds of storms occurred as a front interacted with very unstable air to produce numerous severe thunderstorms that caused extensive hail and wind damage over portions of the area. Hail larger than golf balls was reported over the south side of Springfield.  As the storms moved from west to east across the area, they transitioned from primarily hail producers to wind producers after passing the Highway 65 corridor.||The second round of storms developed near Topeka, KS and roared southeastward into east-central Missouri in the early afternoon. These storms then impacted the eastern Ozarks during the late afternoon hours, producing substantial straight-line wind damage.  The Lebanon Airport anemometer recorded a wind speed of 89 mph before going offline.  There were several reports of tornadoes associated with this second line of storms but none could be confirmed.||The final round of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and evening along a warm front over southeast Kansas and far southwest Missouri, producing primarily large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system became a cut-off low over the Southern Plains, leading to multiple rounds of storms from the latter half of Memorial Day Weekend into much of the following work week. On the afternoon of the 24th, strong storms with hail up to the size of ping pong balls and isolated heavy rainfall developed mainly along and east of Highway 65.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system became cut off over the Southern Plains leading to multiple rounds of storms from the latter half of Memorial Day Weekend into much of the following work week. On the early morning of the 25th, a storm complex that developed over eastern Oklahoma drifted into southwest Missouri. A similar pattern was seen from the afternoon of the 25th to the morning of the 27th, with scattered strong to severe afternoon storms and overnight storm complexes developing near the nose of the low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads in Neosho and in the southern part of the county were flooded and barricaded. The Neosho Fire Department evacuated people from along Hickory Creek due to rapidly rising flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wilsons Creek at Farm Road 146 near Rutledge Park had at least six inches of water over the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved out of southeastern Kansas and produced a swath of hail across Jasper County from Waco to Avilla. Hail from quarter to baseball in size were reported along the path of the storms. In Waco, as the storm entered Missouri, half dollar hail was reported. In Purcell, golf ball to tennis ball hail lasted for nearly 30 minutes causing damage to vehicles and homes. The hail continued across the county impacting Oronogo, where golf ball sized hail occurred, to the eastern edge of the county where ping pong to baseball sized hail fell in Avilla damaging homes and breaking windows out of vehicles.||Damage estimates ranged considerably, partially due to the widespread nature of the hail as well as multiple events over successive days.

As the storms moved from west to east across the area, they transitioned from primarily hail producers to wind producers after passing the Highway 65 corridor.||The second round of storms developed near Topeka, KS and roared southeastward into east-central Missouri in the early afternoon. These storms then impacted the eastern Ozarks during the late afternoon hours, producing substantial straight-line wind damage.  The Lebanon Airport anemometer recorded a wind speed of 89 mph before going offline.  There were several reports of tornadoes associated with this second line of storms but none could be confirmed.||The final round of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and evening along a warm front over southeast Kansas and far southwest Missouri, producing primarily large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were uprooted by high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of training thunderstorms set up across southern Missouri. Several rounds of storms developed over central Kansas and tracked southeastward into the Ozarks as moisture lifted up and over a warm front over the Red River Valley. Many low water crossings flooded over southern Missouri. Highway C along Flat Creek in eastern Barry County and Highway 97 at the Spring River were closed due to flooding. Rainfall accumulations exceeded two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department measured 2.00 inches of rain over a 6 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable air mass interacted with several upper level disturbances and a very slow moving upper level low pressure system to bring heavy rain to the region. The storms occurring on the afternoon of the 14th produced hail larger than golf balls and damaging straight-line winds from Springfield eastward into West Plains. With grounds already saturated from the rainfall on the 11th and 12th,  widespread stream flooding and flash flooding developed, mainly on the 16th and 17, after rainfall accumulations exceeded three inches across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 35 head of cattle were swept down stream as waters rapidly rose in the river valley. Many of the cattle were recovered but at least 2 were found dead down river.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway B was closed at the Panther Creek low water crossing due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering heavy rains continued to produce flash flooding and areal flooding across the region as slow moving storms moved over areas that had received over 3 inches of rain in some instances.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 528 was flooded at the Lick Creek low water crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system became cut off over the Southern Plains leading to multiple rounds of storms from the latter half of Memorial Day Weekend into much of the following work week. On the early morning of the 25th, a storm complex that developed over eastern Oklahoma drifted into southwest Missouri. A similar pattern was seen from the afternoon of the 25th to the morning of the 27th, with scattered strong to severe afternoon storms and overnight storm complexes developing near the nose of the low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed near the intersection of Highway K and Packet Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season snow storm brought heavy wet snow to portions of extreme northeast Missouri, southeast Iowa, and northwest Illinois April 16 and 17. With warm ground temperatures, there were not many impacts to travel other than reduced visibility at times, and minor slushy accumulations on roads. 3 to 6 inches of snow was reported across Scotland and Clark Counties. Higher amounts fell across southeast Iowa, where 5 to 8 inches of snow were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from a public report of 3.5 inches in Kahoka to 6.0 inches of snow 3 miles east northeast of Charlie Heath Memorial Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved over the region from the early morning hours to late afternoon. The first round developed shortly after midnight along an elevated warm front moving northward from the Red River Valley into southwest Missouri. The second round of storms, associated with a meso-scale convective system, developed over western Kansas during the late afternoon of the 21st and moved across southwestern Missouri from mid-morning through late afternoon on the 22nd. Rainfall accumulations over an inch and a half occurred for some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall between 2 and 4 inches occurred across Greene County. West of Springfield, several feet of water was reported over Farm Road 146 at Wilsons Creek and at the Haseltine Branch near Farm Roads 140 and 115 closing the roads to traffic. On Chestnut Expressway near Haseltine Road, 2 vehicles were stalled in 2.5 feet of water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service office in measured 3.80 inches of rainfall over a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system became cut off over the Southern Plains leading to multiple rounds of storms from the latter half of Memorial Day Weekend into much of the following work week. On the early morning of the 25th, a storm complex that developed over eastern Oklahoma drifted into southwest Missouri. A similar pattern was seen from the afternoon of the 25th to the morning of the 27th, with scattered strong to severe afternoon storms and overnight storm complexes developing near the nose of the low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 126 was closed at the North Fork of the Spring River low water crossing due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system became a cut-off low over the Southern Plains, leading to multiple rounds of storms from the latter half of Memorial Day Weekend into much of the following work week. On the afternoon of the 24th, strong storms with hail up to the size of ping pong balls and isolated heavy rainfall developed mainly along and east of Highway 65.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail and winds gusting up to 50 mph was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported that numerous roads were closed throughout Taney County as waters rose quickly due to heavy rain then receded almost as quickly. The river gauge at Bull Creek near Walnut Shade, MO also reported the river was out of banks and above minor flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of three rounds of storms occurred as a front interacted with very unstable air to produce numerous severe thunderstorms that caused extensive hail and wind damage over portions of the area. Hail larger than golf balls was reported over the south side of Springfield.  As the storms moved from west to east across the area, they transitioned from primarily hail producers to wind producers after passing the Highway 65 corridor.||The second round of storms developed near Topeka, KS and roared southeastward into east-central Missouri in the early afternoon. These storms then impacted the eastern Ozarks during the late afternoon hours, producing substantial straight-line wind damage.  The Lebanon Airport anemometer recorded a wind speed of 89 mph before going offline.  There were several reports of tornadoes associated with this second line of storms but none could be confirmed.||The final round of severe storms developed in the late afternoon and evening along a warm front over southeast Kansas and far southwest Missouri, producing primarily large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.
