EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into the Mid-South during the evening hours of April 8, 2020, into the early morning hours of April 9, 2020. The front intersected an area of moderate instability and strong wind shear. As a result, storms developed across eastern Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel then tracked across the Mid-South. Initially, storms were supercellular in nature with large hail and a couple of weak tornadoes. Storms eventually transitioned into clusters and line segments as they moved across the Mississippi River into West Tenessee and North Mississippi. These storms continued to produce large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong storms moved through northeast Missouri the afternoon of April 11, 2020. Nickel size hail was reported with these storms.
As a weak low pressure system moved northeast through the region, some severe convection developed. Some of the storms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind equipment at the Cuba EOC recorded a 58 mph wind gust as a line of severe storms moved through town. Also, a small shed was blown into a street and numerous small tree limbs were blown down around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front triggered showers and storms through the afternoon and into the overnight hours. Some of the storms produced up to four inches of rain causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to four inches of rain fell in about a two and a half hour time period causing flash flooding. Several roads were flooded in and around the Wright City area, including the intersection of State Highways M and H. One road, Roelker Road south of intersection with Appaloosa Way, was completely washed out from the flash flood. Also, Edgewater Circle was flooded in several locations and sustained minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation and flash flooding event occurred over east central Missouri and southwest Illinois beginning during the evening of August 8th, and ending during the early afternoon of August 9th.  Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches with as much as 7 inches were reported, primarily in the St. Louis Metropolitan Area. There were numerous road closures, and at least 13 water rescues.  There were no reported injuries or deaths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of rain fell in about a three hour period causing flash flooding, especially across the far southern portions of the county. Numerous roads were flooded in this area including the intersection of Highway 79 and Highway C.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS developed over western Missouri and moved southeast through forecast area. There were several reports of damaging winds and large hail with some of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees in Brazito. Also, a large tree was uprooted on Henwick Lane on the west side of Jefferson City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation and flash flooding event occurred over east central Missouri and southwest Illinois beginning during the evening of August 8th, and ending during the early afternoon of August 9th.  Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches with as much as 7 inches were reported, primarily in the St. Louis Metropolitan Area. There were numerous road closures, and at least 13 water rescues.  There were no reported injuries or deaths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of rain fell in about a four hour period causing widespread flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded across the county, including Highway 79 at Vomund Road, Winghaven Blvd and Highway N, and Belleau Creek Road just south of intersection with Veterans Memorial Parkway.  At least 5 motorists drove into the flood waters across the county, became stuck and had to be rescued.  Also, the first floor of a two story apartment building on Riverview Lane (just north of the intersection with Highway 94) in St. Charles, MO was flooded with several feet of water.  Residents from 12 of the units had to be evacuated from the apartment building. Several basements were flooded and at least one home near Belleau Creek had several feet of water in it. No injuries or deaths were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 7 inches of rain fell in about a four hour period causing flash flooding, especially across the northern half of the county. Numerous roads were flooded and at least 13 water rescues were performed after vehicles were driven into flood waters. One of the rescues involved two vehicles that were swept away as a creek had risen well out of its banks near the intersection of Conway and White Roads. Three total occupants were rescued. No injuries or deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system ejected eastward into the central part of the country just after Christmas. Precipitation started out as snow, but as warm air aloft moved in it switched over to liquid rain, freezing on contact with all sub-freezing surfaces at ground level.  The worst of the  freezing rain formed generally along the HWY 36 corridor, producing numerous power outages and causing several vehicle accidents. One such accident in Macon county proved fatal as a car slid off an icy road and killed one of its occupants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the day on December 29, a potent winter storm moved into the area. The precipitation started as primarily snow during the morning hours producing a couple inches of accumulation, but switched to freezing rain just before 10 am as warm air aloft moved over the area. Moderate, to at times heavy rain ensued through the rest of the morning and early to middle afternoon hours, before eventually moving out by the evening hours. The main impact from this storm was several power outages around the area. Due to the rain rates, not all of the nearly 1 inch of liquid precipitation accreted on surfaces, but a quarter to half inch did accrete, causing a significant disruption to the power, and closing numerous roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front stalled across the Mid-South started to lift north as low pressure tracked into southwest Missouri. A quasi-stationary baroclinic zone served as the focus for the strongest low-level moisture advection for this event. Also, model guidance suggested that there could be heavier bands of precipitation. However, it did not materialize, thus the snowfall amounts were less than expected for majority of forecast area that was under a Winter Storm Warning. We did have several areas of 3 to 5 inches of snow, especially over portions of central Missouri. Osage County was the only area that received pockets of snow of at least 6 inches with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow fell across Osage County. Numerous roads were snow covered and slick.

A strong system ejected eastward into the central part of the country just after Christmas. Precipitation started out as snow, but as warm air aloft moved in it switched over to liquid rain, freezing on contact with all sub-freezing surfaces at ground level.  The worst of the  freezing rain formed generally along the HWY 36 corridor, producing numerous power outages and causing several vehicle accidents. One such accident in Macon county proved fatal as a car slid off an icy road and killed one of its occupants.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the day on December 29, a potent winter storm moved into the area. The precipitation started as primarily snow during the morning hours producing a couple inches of accumulation, but switched to freezing rain just before 10 am as warm air aloft moved over the area. Moderate, to at times heavy rain ensued through the rest of the morning and early to middle afternoon hours, before eventually moving out by the evening hours. The main impact from this storm was several power outages around the area. Due to the rain rates, not all of the nearly 1 inch of liquid precipitation accreted on surfaces, but a quarter to half inch did accrete, causing a significant disruption to the power, and closing numerous roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated strong to severe storm produced a report of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs up to 3 inches in diameter were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the region on the 26th and meandered across the region through the 31st before an unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system moved east of the region. A very moist airmass and slow movement of the upper level system led to periods of very heavy rainfall each day. The most notable heavy rain event occurred across St. Clair County between Osceola and Lowry City. Over 7 inches of rain fell in a period of a few hours during the early morning of the 31st and led to flash flooding which washed out several roads and bridges and shut down the south bound lane of Highway 13. One public observer from Osceola measured over 11 inches of rain from 3 AM to 9 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several city streets were reported as flooded and impassable along Highway 52 at Newton and DeKalb Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 29-31, a mid level trough dug into the central part of the CONUS, and produced several rounds of moderate to heavy rain. The first day of this parade, the 29th, saw at least one confirmed brief tornado in the Smithville area. There was no damage from this tornado, as it was confirmed via video, not damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak storm moving north exhibited some enhanced low level rotation as it crossed a east to west oriented surface boundary. Several reports came to the office about a potential brief tornado in the Smithville area. Some video evidence was submitted and it was confirmed to be a brief tornado that caused no damage to the Smithville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged south into the Mid-South during the day on May 3, 2020. A potent, quick-moving shortwave interacted with this front and convection developed across Missouri by noon. The airmass across the Mid-South was moderately unstable with fairly strong wind shear in place. The convection formed into a bow echo and intensified as it raced across the northern Mid-South during the afternoon hours. Damaging winds were widespread. By early evening additional storms developed across West Tennessee and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down across Malden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front stalled across the Mid-South started to lift north as low pressure tracked into southwest Missouri. Meanwhile, a potent upper-level disturbance tracked across the region and triggered thunderstorms across Missouri. The airmass across the Mid-South was ripe for severe storms with strong shear and instability in place. Clusters of severe storms moved into northern parts of the Mid-South by mid-afternoon and moved east-southeast through the evening hours producing damaging winds and hail. A macroburst occurred in the Missouri Bootheel producing widespread wind damage. This was the second day in a row that northern sections of the Mid-South suffered widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A macroburst struck portions of Dunklin County with widespread wind damage from Campbell to Clarkton. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down. There was significant damage to a building in Campbell.  A warehouse suffered major damage in Clarkton and a center pivot irrigation system was overturned. The Lynn Oak Church also suffered damage.  A train was derailed along Highway 62 near the Arkansas state line, closing the highway.

Clusters of severe storms moved into northern parts of the Mid-South by mid-afternoon and moved east-southeast through the evening hours producing damaging winds and hail. A macroburst occurred in the Missouri Bootheel producing widespread wind damage. This was the second day in a row that northern sections of the Mid-South suffered widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The macroburst that struck Dunklin and caused widespread damage in southern New Madrid County then spread into northern Pemiscot County causing widespread damage in Wardell. Trees fell on houses. Numerous power lines were knocked down. A mobile home was blown over and several buildings were damaged.  Several homes suffered roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity during the afternoon along an east to west corridor across southeast Missouri into western Kentucky. The storms produced very heavy rainfall, along with an isolated damaging wind gust or two. The storms formed along and ahead of a slow moving frontal boundary that extended generally along the Ohio River into southeast Missouri. The environment near and south of the front was characterized by dew points in the lower to middle 70s and precipitable water values of 1.75 inches. With daytime heating, instability increased substantially to around 3000 J/kg south of the front, with a sharp gradient along the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few small trees and tree limbs were blown down. One tree limb punctured a hole through the roof of a house before striking the floor of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated small clusters of thunderstorms formed during the heat of the afternoon. A 500 mb trough digging southeast across the Great Lakes region forced a cold front southeastward into southern Illinois by evening. The storms ahead of this front produced locally heavy rain and isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over Highway 61 at M Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level high pressure prevailed across the region resulting in high heat and humidity. Heat index values at 105 degrees and greater were widespread on July 15, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values reached 105 to 110 degrees in the afternoon and early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A season-ending hard freeze brought an end to the growing season. A strong high pressure system with a central pressure of 1034 mb was parked over the middle Mississippi Valley early on the 2nd. Clear skies and light winds contributed to low temperatures ranging from the lower 20's north and northwest of Cape Girardeau to 30 degrees around Sikeston and points south and east.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As storms were moving through the region, four men were struck by lightning while at Marquette Park in the Dutchtown neighborhood of St. Louis.  A witness said that the lightning bolt struck one man directly and the others were impacted by the force. Two were able to begin performing CPR on the one who was directly hit. All four were rushed to area hospitals with one person being in critical condition. The man in critical condition died 11 days later on July 30th. He was 28 years old.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to six inches of rain fell in a about a two hour period causing flash flooding. Numerous roads were closed including U.S. Highway 50. Apartments were flooded near the intersection of McCarty Street and East High Street and ones located on nearby Christopher Place due to Gray's Creek rising well out of its banks. This forced the residents to evacuate both complexes. Nine water rescues were performed in Jefferson City after vehicles were driven into the flood waters and stalled.  Area around U.S. Highway 50 and Missouri Blvd., as well as The Millbottom Area, were flooded when Wears Creek rose well above its banks. Also, Wears Creek flooding affected the Washington Park and Vivion Field areas. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices peaked from 105 to 110 degrees in the afternoon ahead of a cold front extending from the St. Louis area to the Ozark Mountains of southwest Missouri.

A cold front moved into the region on the 26th and meandered across the region through the 31st before an unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system moved east of the region. A very moist airmass and slow movement of the upper level system led to periods of very heavy rainfall each day. The most notable heavy rain event occurred across St. Clair County between Osceola and Lowry City. Over 7 inches of rain fell in a period of a few hours during the early morning of the 31st and led to flash flooding which washed out several roads and bridges and shut down the south bound lane of Highway 13. One public observer from Osceola measured over 11 inches of rain from 3 AM to 9 AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall was reported from several locations across Hickory County. Near Galmey, an amateur radio operator reported 4.40 inches of rain falling between 6 am and noon. The Co-op observer at Pomme De Terre reported 2.20 inches of rainfall and a weather watcher measured a 24 hour rainfall total of 5.52 inches near Cross Timers.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding.  Lakeshore Drive around Indian Lake was impassable which cutoff access to parts of the community. Numerous other roads in the area were flooded as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain caused flash flooding in and around the Spalding area. Water two to three feet deep was over Highway M about 3.5 miles west of U.S. Highway 61. Also, a vehicle was stuck in water up to 18 inches deep on Highway H just south of intersection with Highway A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front moved south through the region. A line of strong to severe storms developed ahead of the cold front. Some of the storms produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage occurred across St. Charles County. In O'Fallon, St. Charles, St. Peters and Havester, numerous large tree limbs were blown down.  A tree was blown over onto Highway DD near Sommers Road.  In St. Charles, one of the large tree limbs fell onto a garage causing minor roof damage. In St. Peters, a tree was blown onto a house causing moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front moved south through the region. A line of strong to severe storms developed ahead of the cold front. Some of the storms produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down up to 40 large trees, numerous tree limbs and power lines across portions of the city.  A power pole was blown down in the 3800 block of Shaw Boulevard. Several unoccupied vehicles sustained damage from fallen trees and tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front moved south through the region. A line of strong to severe storms developed ahead of the cold front. Some of the storms produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage occurred across St. Louis County, especially across the central portions between Maryland Heights and Oakville.  Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. In Maryland Heights, a large tree was blown down onto power lines near the intersection of Ameling Road and Bennington Place. Several unoccupied vehicles sustained damage from fallen trees and tree limbs. On vehicle was destroyed by a fallen tree in Rock Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low centered over Missouri moved slowly eastward with two lobes of vorticity that were pivoting around each other. The interaction of the two vort maxes produced two distinct areas of heavy rain, one over central/east central Missouri and another over south central and southeast Missouri. Very heavy rain fell in these areas, mainly between 3 and 4 inches of rain, though a few areas had around 6 to 7 inches of rain with this system. There were several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Several roads were flooded including Highway 100 near Gasconade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours, the tail end of a mesoscale convective complex moved southeastward from the Central Plains into central and south-central Missouri through the early afternoon. Heavy rain fell in a band from Warsaw through Marshfield to Gainesville, with accumulations of one to two inches. Isolated reports of thunderstorm wind damage to trees and power lines were received from Camden, Pulaski, Ozark, and Phelps counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a power line. The weather station in Camdenton reco0rded a maximum wind gust of 43 mph.

Severe straight-line winds produced damage to trees and powerlines in Taney, Stone, McDonald and Jasper counties in southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down east of the Northpark Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed as a mesoscale convective vorticity maximum (MCV) over eastern Kansas moved into the Missouri Ozarks and interacted with very moist and unstable air.  A couple of these storms were strong enough to produce nickel sized hail  and damaging wind gusts. Localized heavy rainfall of one and a half to three inches also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage was reported to a small area along Rinehart Road, Shary View Road, including the Savannah Place and Stephens Estates subdivisions, just east of highway 65 and just south of highway F, north of Branson. Power was reported out to around 400 homes. Limbs up to 8 inches in diameter and several power poles and lines were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of broken lines of thunderstorms moved southeast across southeast Missouri during the heat of the afternoon. The storms occurred ahead of a cold front that extended from central Illinois to west central Missouri. Precipitable water values ranged from 1.5 to 1.9 inches, which resulted in locally heavy downpours and isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of several low water crossings was reported in and north of Van Buren.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several city streets around Doniphan were closed by flash flooding. The automated observing system at the Doniphan fire weather station measured 2.11 inches in an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area with a line of storms developing along it and producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a 12 inch diameter tree blocking New London Gravel Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a large tree over onto Highway W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of storm activity and heavy rainfall affected the region during the day. An upper level low pressure system sparked thunderstorm activity during the morning and afternoon over central and south-central Missouri.  Additional storms developed over southwest Missouri during the afternoon as the moist and unstable air mass interacted with outflow from the morning storms and a stalled front along the Arkansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Highway 87 and Business 54 was closed with over a foot of water across the intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large but loosely-organized complex of thunderstorms moved southeast across southeast Missouri during the afternoon. Storms produced heavy rain, along with isolated wind damage in the Ozark foothills. The storms blossomed in a convergent wind flow south of a closed 850 mb low over northern Missouri. Daytime heating contributed to moderately strong instability, with mixed-layer cape values of 2000 to 2500 j/kg. Precipitable water values from 1.75 to 2 inches supported heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were down, and one tree was snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee reported a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and storms dropped up to 4 inches of rain in a short amount of time in the St. Louis metro area causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell in a 3 hour period which caused flash flooding. Highway 141 at the intersection with I-44 was flooded in both the north and south bound lanes. Also, several creeks, including Yarnell and Williams creeks, rose above flood stage causing a number of road closures in the immediate area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed southeast Missouri, mainly south through southwest of the Perryville area. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less around sunrise. The fog formed in an area of light winds between high pressure over the southern Plains states and a stationary front over the Appalachian Mountains.

With the influence of daytime heating, scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed ahead of an upper-level disturbance moving east along the eastern Kansas and Oklahoma border. The organization of the storms was aided by a weak surface boundary in the vicinity of the Missouri and Arkansas border. Deep southwesterly flow ahead of the upper-level disturbance supported tropical precipitable water values of two inches or greater. In addition to ample moisture and instability, slow storm motions raised the flash flooding potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creeks were all out of their banks across the far northwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front moved south through the region. A line of strong to severe storms developed ahead of the cold front. Some of the storms produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded. About a foot and a half of water was over Paris Road near Brown Station Road.

Scattered slow-moving thunderstorms during the afternoon produced heavy rainfall. Hourly totals of one to two inches resulted in isolated flooding. The storms were associated with an upper-level disturbance moving eastward across the mid Mississippi Valley. At the surface, a weak area of low pressure was near the Missouri/Kentucky border. The environment was characterized by dew points around 70 with precipitable water values near 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were covered with water in low spots. Approximately two inches of rain fell in less than one hour.

Also, about 3 miles east northeast of Troy, large tree limbs were blown down and part of a tin roof of a east-west facing gable barn was torn off. In Foley, several large trees were uprooted.

During the late morning into early afternoon on August 10 a roaring derecho caused widespread damage across central and eastern Iowa. While the main impacts of this disastrous storm remained just north of the Iowa and Missouri border some of the outflow winds dove southward into northern Missouri causing sporadic damage to trees and structures. Wind speeds in northern Missouri were generally in the 60 to 70 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and powerlines were blown down across the Blythedale and Eagleville areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low drifted slowly eastward across the northern Plains, with a band of moderately strong southwesterly flow aloft overspreading portions of the mid Mississippi Valley. A cold front also moved eastward across this region, providing the focus for a line of showers and a few thunderstorms during the evening. Vertical wind shear was sufficient for some convective organization, with gusty winds. However, weak temperature lapse rates limited the coverage and intensity of strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of the 10th as an upper level disturbance and outflow boundary from an intense derecho over Iowa interacted with a stalled front. The strongest storms produced hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter. Strong straight line winds produced damage across the region. During the early morning hours of the 11th, another upper level disturbance generated additional storms mainly over areas along and south of Interstate 44.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were damaged or downed in the city of Houston. A tree fell through a garage causing severe damage with several other outbuildings also suffering tree damage across town. Winds were estimated at 70mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a utility pole causing a transformer to fail and power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little rain fell in September, further impacting the drought that began in August. The 30/60/90 Day precipitation amounts continued to decline with Severe (D2) drought expanding across portions of the Ozarks. Low stream flows were noted on some area rivers and KBDI indices rose indicating enhanced fire weather concerns. Some indication in the agricultural community were beginning to occur with some farmers starting feeding hay about a month early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With only 1/4 to 1 inch of rain falling during the month, Severe (D2) Drought expanded into Barry County in September.   Impacts from the drought will be complied and included in the final drought narrative when conditions improve.

During the early morning hours of the 11th, another upper level disturbance generated additional storms mainly over areas along and south of Interstate 44.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the end of August the combination of warm temperatures, lack of rainfall over the previous 30-90 days and falling stream levels worked together to produce Severe drought conditions across portions of  Greene, Christian, Stone, Webster, Douglas and Lawrence counties. Drought conditions continued into September. Final drought impacts will be included in the last drought entry when the drought has ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of warm temperatures, lack of rainfall during the summer, and falling stream levels worked together to produce Severe drought conditions. Rainfall amounts the last 90 days were generally in the 4-8 inch range which was 4 to 8 inches below normal for that period. Final drought impacts will be included in the last drought entry when the drought has ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms occurred during the morning hours. A 500 mb disturbance over north central Arkansas moved northeast, initiating heavy rain just north of an outflow boundary stretching from north central Arkansas into southern Oklahoma. Rainfall amounts of an inch or more occurred over very moist ground, resulting in localized stream flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management personnel submitted pictures of street flooding in Van Buren and water flowing over a low water crossing near Van Buren. A city street in Ellsinore was closed at a low water crossing bridge due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful line of severe thunderstorms produced a derecho which tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Monday, August 10th, resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. The cost of damage from this storm was extreme, reaching an estimated 7.5 billion dollars along the path of these storms from Iowa through Illinois.  Two brief tornadoes have been confirmed within the widespread swath of wind damage, one just north of the Eastern Iowa Airport in Linn County Iowa, and one just south of Freeport, Illinois. These tornadoes have been designated as EF-U, as there was no observable damage directly attributable to the tornadoes from which an EF-scale rating could be assigned. While an average of two derechos a year occur across the area, a derecho of this intensity is a roughly once-in-a-decade occurrence. Another unusual aspect of this derecho was the duration of the strong winds, with gusts over 60 mph for over an hour at some locations.||The most extreme winds, estimated at 110-140 mph, destroyed or damaged numerous outbuildings, barns, grain bins, homes, mobile homes, apartment buildings, trees, and power poles in parts of Benton, Linn, Jones, Cedar, and Clinton Counties. The Cedar Rapids area was particularly hard hit. One fatality occurred in Linn County, as a tree fell on a cyclist. Several homes, apartment complexes, and businesses sustained damage consistent with 130-140 mph winds. Radio transmission towers in Marion and Clinton, Iowa collapsed due to winds estimated around 130 mph. Wind gusts of 80-100 mph were common as the line of storms moved through the Quad Cities area and then through northwest Illinois. A small pocket of winds estimated at 100-110 mph impacted Princeton, IL in Bureau County, where a 150 foot communications tower collapsed and numerous power poles were snapped. In addition to the damage, numerous long-duration power outages occurred across the region. Damage to crops was considerable along the derecho path. Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported trees blown down in Memphis due to outflow winds ahead of the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little rain fell in September, further impacting the drought that began in August. The 30/60/90 Day precipitation amounts continued to decline with Severe (D2) drought expanding across portions of the Ozarks. Low stream flows were noted on some area rivers and KBDI indices rose indicating enhanced fire weather concerns. Some indication in the agricultural community were beginning to occur with some farmers starting feeding hay about a month early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall remained sparse in September with most locations receiving only 1/4 to 1.5 inches   or 10-25% of normal across a large portion of Lawrence County, further impacting the Severe (D2) Drought that began following a dry summer.  Impacts from the drought will be complied and included in the final drought narrative when conditions improve.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system tracked across the Missouri Bootheel and northwest Tennessee and into the TN River River by mid-afternoon on September 1st. In the wake of the departing mesoscale convective system, an isolated thunderstorm developed over the Missouri Bootheel and moved into Lake County, Tennessee. A couple of weak tornadoes occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed just south of Deering. The tornado moved east northeast on an intermittent path.  The tornado moved across open fields, and no structural damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front combined with an upper level disturbance to generate slow moving thunderstorms. This produced areas of very heavy rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in about a one to two hour period causing flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded including the Forest Park Parkway exit to Market Street, as well as, the intersection of Vandeventer and Delmar Blvd, and a section of Lindell Blvd. just west of Vandeventer Avenue. Several cars stalled out after being driven into the flooded sections of roadways around the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front as it sliced eastward across Missouri. Gusts over 40 mph were recorded in the Mississippi River delta, from New Madrid up to Cape Girardeau. The highest measured gust was 54 mph at a mesonet site near Charleston in Mississippi County. The peak gust at the Cape Girardeau airport was 43 mph.

Strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front as it sliced eastward across Missouri. Gusts over 40 mph were recorded in the Mississippi River delta, from New Madrid up to Cape Girardeau. The highest measured gust was 54 mph at a mesonet site near Charleston in Mississippi County. The peak gust at the Cape Girardeau airport was 43 mph.

A strong cold front moved across the region during the late afternoon and evening, and interacted with an unstable air mass and strong vertical wind shear to produce several rounds of strong to severe storms. Widespread wind gusts over 60 mph were reported across the region. The greatest damage occurred near Osage Beach and St. Robert, where straight-line wind gusts over 70 mph produced wind damage at a strip mall and damaged or destroyed manufactured homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level disturbances passed over the frontal zone and interacted with increasing low level moisture to produce several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall from the evening of the 20th to the pre-dawn hours of the 22nd. Rainfall accumulations for the 48-hour period ending at 3 AM on the 22nd ranged from one to two inches, with the highest totals over the eastern Ozarks. Localized flooding of low water crossings over southwest and south central Missouri led to two fatalities during the early morning of the 22nd. The first fatality occurred in Wright County at AB Highway north of Mansfield along Wolf Creek. An 18-year old female drove into the flooded low water crossing at around 12:30 AM, and drowned while trying to escape her vehicle. The second fatality occurred in Dent County around 5 AM. A driver with a six-year old passenger drove into the flooded low water crossing on Highway TT at Crooked Creek northeast of Salem. The driver was able to swim to safety, but the 6-year old was swept downstream and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newburg Creek flooded a section of Water Street at the low water crossing. The crossing was closed to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front moved south through the region. A line of strong to severe storms developed ahead of the cold front. Some of the storms produced widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms moved across Jefferson County. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down.  In Pevely, a 51 year old woman was killed when a tree was uprooted by the thunderstorm winds and it fell on her. She had been helping her boyfriend move a hard top from a Jeep to prevent damage to it from the approaching severe storms.  The boyfriend was not injured by the fallen tree.  In Arnold, a large maple tree was blown over onto a house. The house suffered damage to the awning and gutters before rolling off the slanted roof.  Just southwest of Festus a tree was blown down onto Route CC. Several unoccupied vehicles sustained damage from fallen trees and tree limbs.

Dry conditions continued through much of the month of October, continuing the drought conditions which began in August. The 30/60/90 Day precipitation amounts continued to decline with Severe (D2) and Extreme (D3) drought developing or expanding across portions of the Ozarks. Low stream flows were noted on some area rivers and KBDI indices rose indicating enhanced fire weather concerns. Some indication in the agricultural community were beginning to occur with some farmers starting feeding hay about a month early. Much of the impacts felt through the increasing drought were limited by the end of the growing season. Primary impacts were limited for the most part to surface and and ground water issues and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Through the first 3 weeks of October, less than half an inch of rain fell across the county. With 30/60 and 90 day percent of normal rainfalls in the 50 percent or less range, severe (D2) drought conditions developed. Despite the very dry conditions, little to no damaging impacts occurred. The primary concerns were focused on ground water and surface water where low stream flows, lakes levels and some impacts to monitoring wells occurred. Agricultural entities saw little impacts as the growing season had effectively ended and harvesting was aided by the dry weather. A pattern shift occurred during the last week of the month bringing significant rainfall and drought conditions improving to moderate (D1) drought by the end of the month. With drought conditions falling below Severe (D2) Drought conditions this will be the last entry for drought in Hickory County.

Surface high pressure was centered over the lower Ohio Valley, resulting in conditions conducive for frost.

The 30/60/90 Day precipitation amounts continued to decline with Severe (D2) and Extreme (D3) drought developing or expanding across portions of the Ozarks. Low stream flows were noted on some area rivers and KBDI indices rose indicating enhanced fire weather concerns. Some indication in the agricultural community were beginning to occur with some farmers starting feeding hay about a month early. Much of the impacts felt through the increasing drought were limited by the end of the growing season. Primary impacts were limited for the most part to surface and and ground water issues and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Through the first 3 weeks of October, less than half an inch of rain fell across the county. With 30/60 and 90 day percent of normal rainfalls in the 50 percent or less range, severe (D2) drought conditions developed. Despite the very dry conditions, little to no damaging impacts occurred. The primary concerns were focused on ground water and surface water where low stream flows, lakes levels and some impacts to monitoring wells occurred. Agricultural entities saw little impacts as the growing season had effectively ended and harvesting was aided by the dry weather. A pattern shift occurred during the last week of the month bringing significant rainfall and drought conditions improving to moderate (D1) drought by the end of the month. With drought conditions falling below Severe (D2) Drought conditions this will be the last entry for drought in Vernon County.

A shortwave lifting northeastward across Missouri and a slow moving mesoscale convective vorticity maximum interacted with a warm and humid air mass across northeast Missouri and brought thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall of 4 to 6 inches and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that water was now coming up over the gravel road (Clark County Road 7) near her house. The spotter also reported that water was coming up over Road U as well which was north of their location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening of October 11 a strong cold front caused several complexes of thunderstorms to form across southeast Nebraska. These storms moved into northwest Missouri. The winds associated with the cold front itself were above 50 mph, so the thunderstorms did not need to contribute much to bring severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report came from the a gas station on the southwest corner of I-29 and the HWY 136 interchange near Rockport, Missouri.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several upper level disturbances passed over the frontal zone and interacted with increasing low level moisture to produce several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall from the evening of the 20th to the pre-dawn hours of the 22nd. Rainfall accumulations for the 48-hour period ending at 3 AM on the 22nd ranged from one to two inches, with the highest totals over the eastern Ozarks. Localized flooding of low water crossings over southwest and south central Missouri led to two fatalities during the early morning of the 22nd. The first fatality occurred in Wright County at AB Highway north of Mansfield along Wolf Creek. An 18-year old female drove into the flooded low water crossing at around 12:30 AM, and drowned while trying to escape her vehicle. The second fatality occurred in Dent County around 5 AM. A driver with a six-year old passenger drove into the flooded low water crossing on Highway TT at Crooked Creek northeast of Salem. The driver was able to swim to safety, but the 6-year old was swept downstream and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway Z was closed due to flooding at Elk Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed over the Central Plains during the evening of the 3rd tracked southeastward into the Missouri Ozarks and southeast Kansas during the early and mid-morning hours of the 4th. The storms|produced wind gusts over 60 mph and caused widespread damage to roofs, trees and power lines from Horton to Branson. In addition to the strong winds, rainfall accumulations over two inches occurred over central Missouri near Rolla, Lebanon and Lake of the Ozarks, and caused flooding and road closures on several county routes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed over the Central Plains during the evening of the 3rd tracked southeastward into the Missouri Ozarks and southeast Kansas during the early and mid-morning hours of the 4th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved from northwest to southeast across Greene County producing straight line winds between 60 and 80 mph. The ASOS at the Springfield/Branson National Airport recorded a 61 mph gust as the storms moved through. Numerous reports were received of large trees uprooted or snapped, large limbs down, power poles and line from the airport, south to Battlefield and southeast to Galloway. Several trees and limbs fell on homes causing minor to moderate roof or siding damage as the storms moved southeast. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over central and west central Missouri during the afternoon and early evening along an outflow boundary from early morning storms over northern Missouri. One storm in Phelps County produced wind gusts strong enough to  blow shingles off a home in Newburg. Rainfall accumulations over two inches inches occurred over Maries and Pulaski counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor roof damage was reported to a home as high winds blew shingles off the home on County Road 7360 near Newburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very weak flow aloft and an anomalously unstable and moist environment along a residual outflow boundary promoted the development of thunderstorms across the mid Mississippi and lower Ohio Valleys through the late afternoon into mid evening. A weak shortwave slowly advanced eastward along the bootheel of Missouri, while a weak impulse moved southeast from the northern border of Missouri. An outflow boundary was the primary focus for renewed development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. The environment was characterized by anomalously high precipitable water values, on the order of 1.8 to 2.1 inches, which is roughly 2 standard deviations above the climatological mean. A strong instability gradient existed as well, upwards of 5000 J/kg surface based cape across southern MO to northwest TN. A southwesterly inflow of 20-30 kts at 850 mb impinged on the boundary, sustaining the high precipitable water values and maximizing the convergence. Rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, and pockets of damaging wind were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were briefly flooded in Perryville. Some county roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms occurred ahead of a cold front that extended from the Great Lakes region southwestward to Kansas. Strong gusty winds and hail accompanied a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was reported at the intersection of Highway E and County Road 208.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large clusters of thunderstorms during the early morning hours produced locally excessive rainfall. The storms occurred ahead of a very compact disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere over areas of far west central Illinois. A shortwave trough axis extended south from the disturbance. The air mass across the region was very moist, with precipitable water values around 2 inches. There was also a pool of moderate to strong instability over central and western Missouri that advanced northeastward with the aid of a 20 to 30 kt southwest low-level jet. Torrential rainfall caused some localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread street flooding occurred in Cape Girardeau. In some low-lying areas, the water was three feet deep. Water was above the tire levels on parked cars. A nursing home was nearly inundated. A rainfall amount of 3.25 inches was measured at Southeast Missouri State University.

The peak gust at the Poplar Bluff airport was 42 mph.

The environment was characterized by anomalously high precipitable water values, on the order of 1.8 to 2.1 inches, which is roughly 2 standard deviations above the climatological mean. A strong instability gradient existed as well, upwards of 5000 J/kg surface based cape across southern MO to northwest TN. A southwesterly inflow of 20-30 kts at 850 mb impinged on the boundary, sustaining the high precipitable water values and maximizing the convergence. Rain rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common, and pockets of damaging wind were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six-inch tree branches were broken off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous low water crossings were flooded west and southwest of Van Buren. Flash flooding was reported along Pike Creek in the Fremont area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of heavy rainfall events in mid and late May caused the Mississippi River to rise above flood stage. The flooding was minor, consisting of flooded fields, low-lying access roads, and some riverside parkland. Some minor crop damage likely occurred. The river fell back below flood stage during the early to mid parts of June.

Several upper level disturbances passed over the frontal zone and interacted with increasing low level moisture to produce several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall from the evening of the 20th to the pre-dawn hours of the 22nd. Rainfall accumulations for the 48-hour period ending at 3 AM on the 22nd ranged from one to two inches, with the highest totals over the eastern Ozarks. Localized flooding of low water crossings over southwest and south central Missouri led to two fatalities during the early morning of the 22nd. The first fatality occurred in Wright County at AB Highway north of Mansfield along Wolf Creek. An 18-year old female drove into the flooded low water crossing at around 12:30 AM, and drowned while trying to escape her vehicle. The second fatality occurred in Dent County around 5 AM. A driver with a six-year old passenger drove into the flooded low water crossing on Highway TT at Crooked Creek northeast of Salem. The driver was able to swim to safety, but the 6-year old was swept downstream and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roubidoux Creek reached minor flood stage causing water to impact Highway 17 in northern Texas County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted northward through Arkansas and into Missouri from New Years Eve into New Years Day. Freezing rain spread into southeast Kansas and southern and central Missouri during the evening hours of Thursday, December 31. The freezing rain continued into January 1, 2021 before transitioning over to minor accumulations of snow.  Ice accumulations overnight and into January 1, 2021 resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flat ice accumulations estimated between 0.15 and 0.25 inches were reported around the county. This storm continued into January 1, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across northern Missouri during the early afternoon of May 24th, and resulted in damaging winds reaching into portions of Scotland County Missouri.  Heavy rainfall amounts of around 2 inches also fell with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a vacant house and barn doors were blown in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado formed near I-29 close to the town of Oregon, Missouri. This tornado was filmed, so while video evidence exists, there was no reported damage from this brief tornado. Ongoing moderate to heavy rain caused some roads to be  closed in central Missouri through most of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route PP was closed near West Locust Creek west of Milan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe storms came through the Kansas City metro area on back to back nights, both taking place late in the evening and lasting through the overnight hours. Both events were nearly identical in evolution, with a strong QLCS each night producing scattered wind damage. The June 4 QLCS produced a couple weak and brief EF-0 tornadoes that did some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter in Warrensburg reported 67 mph wind with a stationary anemometer mounted to the vehicle.
